title: 10 best herbal teas for cough
intro_desc: Herbal teas for cough are infusions made from various plants, flowers, and spices, which can help alleviate symptoms of coughs and colds. These teas often contain natural anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties that can soothe irritated throats, reduce inflammation, and help expel mucus. Examples of herbal teas that help with cough include thyme, licorice root, slippery elm, and eucalyptus. By drinking these teas, individuals can find relief from coughing fits, leading to improved sleep, increased productivity, and an overall better quality of life. For instance, a study published in the Journal of Ethnopharmacology found that thyme tea was as effective as a common cough syrup in relieving acute bronchitis symptoms, highlighting the potential benefits of herbal teas for cough.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon verbena tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the throat and help alleviate irritation, making it easier to cough up mucus and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. Additionally, lemon verbena is rich in antioxidants that boost the immune system, helping the body fight off infections and speed up recovery. Furthermore, the tea's soothing and calming effects can help reduce stress and promote better sleep, which is essential for a quicker recovery from a cough.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Lemon verbena tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel. This is due to the presence of verbascoside, a compound that has been shown to have expectorant properties.

Antitussive: Lemon verbena tea has the ability to suppress coughing, which can provide relief to individuals experiencing a persistent cough. This is attributed to the presence of various flavonoids, such as luteolin and apigenin, which have been shown to have antitussive effects.

Antimicrobial: Lemon verbena tea has the ability to inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses, which can help to reduce the severity and duration of a cough. This is due to the presence of various compounds, such as citral, geraniol, and limonene, which have antimicrobial properties.

Anti-inflammatory: Lemon verbena tea has the ability to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can help to alleviate coughing. This is attributed to the presence of various compounds, such as verbascoside and luteolin, which have anti-inflammatory effects.

Bronchodilator: Lemon verbena tea can help to widen the airways in the lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing the severity of a cough. This is due to the presence of various compounds, such as verbascoside and limonene, which have bronchodilator effects.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the lemon verbena plant are rich in essential oils and have anti-spasmodic properties, making them effective in soothing coughs.
Flowers: The flowers of the lemon verbena plant contain flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce irritation in the throat, alleviating cough.
Stems: The stems of the lemon verbena plant contain high levels of rosmarinic acid, which has been shown to have expectorant properties, making it useful in treating coughs.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 2 cups of water and 1 tablespoon of dried lemon verbena leaves, bringing them to a boil.
Reduce heat to low, cover the pot, and let the mixture simmer for 10 minutes to allow the herb to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the spent lemon verbena leaves.
Add honey or lemon juice to taste, if desired, for added soothing and flavor.
Sip the hot tea slowly, allowing it to coat your throat and alleviate coughing.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes slippery when it comes into contact with water. This mucilage coats the throat and soothes irritation, reducing the urge to cough. Additionally, it is believed to have anti-tussive and expectorant properties, which help to expel mucus from the respiratory tract and alleviate congestion. Furthermore, marshmallow tea has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to reduce inflammation in the airways and ease breathing. Its soothing and calming effects make it an ideal natural remedy for coughs and sore throats.
remedy_properties: Demulcent: Marshmallow tea is rich in mucilage, which forms a soothing film over the mucous membranes in the respiratory tract, providing relief from irritation, inflammation, and coughing.
Expectorant: The mucilage in marshmallow tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and expel phlegm.
Anti-inflammatory: Marshmallow tea contains flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have anti-inflammatory properties, reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviating coughing.
Antitussive: The soothing effect of marshmallow tea on the respiratory tract can help to suppress coughing, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits.

Antimicrobial: Marshmallow tea has antimicrobial properties due to its polysaccharides, which can help to prevent the growth of bacteria and viruses that can cause respiratory infections, reducing the likelihood of coughing.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in triterpene glycosides, which have been shown to have anti-tussive properties, making them effective in soothing coughs.
Flowers: Contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, helping to alleviate irritation in the respiratory tract.
Stems: Packed with coumarins and phenolic acids, which can help reduce inflammation and mucus production, providing relief for coughs and congestion.
Roots: A natural source of asparagin, which has been found to have expectorant properties, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and phlegm.
Bark: Contains high levels of tannins and polyphenols, which have astringent properties, helping to soothe irritated throat tissues and alleviate coughing.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water, 2 tablespoons of dried marshmallow root, and 1 tablespoon of thyme leaves; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover, and simmer the mixture for 10-15 minutes.
Remove from heat and add 1 tablespoon of dried elderflower and 1 tablespoon of dried peppermint leaves; let steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea, then add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day as needed for cough relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Daisy tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the throat and reduce irritation. The herbal tea's natural healing compounds, such as flavonoids and sesquiterpene lactones, work together to alleviate coughing and ease congestion. Additionally, daisy tea has expectorant properties that help clear phlegm and mucus from the respiratory tract, providing relief from persistent coughs and making breathing easier. Its soothing and calming effects on the nervous system also help reduce coughing fits, promoting relaxation and restful sleep.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Daisy tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel phlegm. This is due to the presence of mucilaginous compounds and flavonoids.
Antitussive: Daisy tea has the ability to suppress coughing, providing relief from constant, unproductive coughing. This is attributed to the presence of sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids.

Anti-inflammatory: Daisy tea has the ability to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating irritation and discomfort. This is due to the presence of anti-inflammatory compounds such as flavonoids and essential oils.

Antimicrobial: Daisy tea has the ability to inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses, helping to prevent and treat respiratory infections. This is attributed to the presence of antimicrobial compounds such as essential oils, sesquiterpene lactones, and flavonoids.

Antioxidant: Daisy tea contains high levels of antioxidants, which help to protect the respiratory tract from damage caused by free radicals. This is due to the presence of antioxidant compounds such as flavonoids and essential oils.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the daisy tea plant contain compounds that help soothe throat irritation and reduce coughing.
Flowers: The flowers of the daisy tea plant have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of cough and cold.
Buds: The buds of the daisy tea plant are rich in antioxidants that can help boost the immune system and provide relief from cough.
Roots: The roots of the daisy tea plant have expectorant properties that can help clear phlegm and provide relief from cough.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the daisy tea plant contain compounds that can help relax the bronchial muscles and provide relief from coughing.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 teaspoons of fresh daisy flowers or 1 teaspoon of dried daisy flowers.
Rinse the daisy flowers under cold water to remove any dirt or impurities.
Place the daisy flowers in a tea infuser or teapot and pour 8 ounces of boiling water over them.
Let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to be released.
Strain the tea, discard the spent flowers, and enjoy the herbal daisy tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day for cough relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold tea helps with cough because it contains anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties that aid in soothing a sore throat and expelling mucus from the respiratory tract. The tea's warm temperature also helps to alleviate congestion and reduce inflammation in the airways, making it easier to breathe. Additionally, pot marigold tea is rich in antioxidants that boost the immune system, helping the body to fight off infections that may cause coughing. Drinking pot marigold tea can provide relief from cough symptoms and promote overall respiratory health.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Pot marigold tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel. This property is important for cough as it helps to relieve congestion and reduce the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids and saponins.

Anti-inflammatory: Pot marigold tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can help to alleviate coughing and soothe irritation. This property is important for cough as inflammation can exacerbate coughing and cause discomfort. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids and volatile oils.

Antimicrobial: Pot marigold tea has antimicrobial properties that can help to fight off infections that may be causing or exacerbating cough. This property is important for cough as infections can lead to persistent and severe coughing fits. The active constituents responsible for this property include volatile oils and alkaloids.

Antitussive: Pot marigold tea has antitussive properties that help to suppress coughing. This property is important for cough as it can help to reduce the frequency and intensity of coughing fits, providing relief and allowing for rest. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids and mucilage.

Demulcent: Pot marigold tea has demulcent properties that help to soothe and protect the mucous membranes of the respiratory tract. This property is important for cough as it can help to alleviate irritation and reduce coughing. The active constituents responsible for this property include mucilage and saponins.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Pot marigold flowers are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing irritated throats and alleviating cough symptoms.
Leaves: The leaves of the pot marigold plant contain flavonoids and carotenoids, which have expectorant and antitussive properties, helping to loosen phlegm and reduce coughing.

Aerial parts: While not a specific part, aerial parts of the pot marigold plant, such as leaves and stems, are often used together in teas for their combined soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on the respiratory system, helping to alleviate cough and other cold symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 2 cups of water and 2 tablespoons of dried pot marigold flowers.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the flowers, leaving only the liquid.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for cough relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cannabis tea helps with cough because it contains cannabinoids, such as THC and CBD, which have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. These compounds can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, relieving cough and irritation. Additionally, cannabis tea can help relax the muscles in the airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing the frequency and severity of coughing fits. The soothing, warm nature of the tea can also help to calm a sore throat and alleviate symptoms of congestion. Furthermore, cannabis tea can help stimulate the release of mucus, making it easier to expel and clear the airways.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Cannabis tea can help relieve coughing by suppressing the cough reflex, thanks to the active constituents such as tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and cannabidiol (CBD).

Anti-inflammatory: The anti-inflammatory properties of cannabis tea can help reduce irritation and swelling in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate coughing, due to the presence of cannabinoids like THC and CBD.
Bronchodilator: Cannabis tea can help expand the airways in the lungs, making it easier to breathe and reducing coughing, as a result of THC and other cannabinoids.
Expectorant: Cannabis tea can help loosen and expel mucus from the lungs, which can reduce coughing and congestion, due to the active constituents like THC and CBD.
Analgesic: The pain-relieving properties of cannabis tea can help alleviate the discomfort associated with coughing, thanks to the presence of cannabinoids such as THC and CBD.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in cannabinoids and terpenes, the leaves help alleviate inflammation and suppress cough.
Flowers: Contain high levels of cannabinoids and essential oils, making them effective in reducing cough and providing relief from respiratory issues.
Seeds: High in essential fatty acids, seeds can help reduce inflammation and soothe a sore throat, thus providing relief from cough.
Buds: Packed with cannabinoids and terpenes, buds offer potent anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, making them ideal for treating cough.
remedy_recipe: Grind 1-2 grams of cannabis flower, preferably a high-CBD strain, and place it in a tea infuser.
Boil 4 cups of water and let it cool down for a few minutes to 200-212°F (93-100°C).
Place the tea infuser with the cannabis in a large teapot and pour the hot water over it.
Steep the cannabis in the hot water for 15-20 minutes, then remove the infuser.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and drink the tea slowly to soothe a cough.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Bell pepper tea helps with cough because it contains a rich amount of vitamin C and antioxidants that work to soothe the throat, reduce inflammation, and boost the immune system. The capsaicin in bell peppers acts as a natural decongestant, helping to clear mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract. Additionally, the tea's warm temperature can provide relief by alleviating congestion and providing comfort to a sore throat. By drinking bell pepper tea, individuals can find relief from their cough and promote a faster recovery.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Bell pepper tea can help alleviate coughing by suppressing the cough reflex, due to its high concentration of carotenoids like beta-carotene and violaxanthin.
Expectorant: The tea can help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, thanks to its rich supply of vitamin C and other antioxidant compounds.

Anti-inflammatory: Bell pepper tea's ability to reduce inflammation in the airways can help soothe irritation and relieve coughing, partly due to the presence of sinapic acid and other phenolic acids.

Immune-boosting: The tea's high vitamin C content and other antioxidants can help strengthen the immune system, making it more effective at fighting off respiratory infections that can cause coughing.

Antimicrobial: Bell pepper tea can help eliminate the bacteria and viruses that can lead to respiratory infections and coughing, in part because of its content of volatile oils and flavonoids with antimicrobial properties.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in essential oils and flavonoids, the leaves help soothe throat irritation and reduce cough.
Flowers: Contain anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in alleviating cough and respiratory issues.
Seeds: Packed with antioxidants and vitamins, seeds help boost the immune system and provide relief from cough and cold symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Chop one organic bell pepper and place it in a pot with four cups of water.
Bring the water to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Add one tablespoon of dried thyme leaves and one tablespoon of dried oregano leaves to the pot.
Allow the herbs to steep for 10 minutes, then strain the tea into a mug.
Sip the tea throughout the day, reheating as necessary, for cough relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cinnamon tea helps with cough because it has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties that soothe the throat and help clear mucus. The spicy flavor of cinnamon stimulates the production of saliva, which keeps the throat lubricated and reduces irritation. Additionally, cinnamon is rich in antioxidants that boost the immune system and help the body fight off infections that can cause coughing. The warm temperature of the tea also helps to relax the muscles in the chest and alleviate coughing spasms. Drinking cinnamon tea can provide quick relief from a persistent cough and promote overall respiratory health.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Cinnamon tea helps alleviate coughs by acting as a natural suppressant for chronic coughs, thanks to its active constituents, cinnamaldehyde and coumarin.
Expectorant: The warming effect of cinnamon tea loosens phlegm and mucus in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel and reducing coughing, due to the presence of volatile oils.

Anti-inflammatory: Cinnamon tea reduces inflammation in the respiratory tract, soothing irritation and alleviating persistent coughing, attributable to the presence of antioxidant compounds like eugenol.
Antimicrobial: Cinnamon tea inhibits bacterial and fungal growth, which can cause or worsen coughs, as a result of the active constituent, cinnamaldehyde.

Immunomodulatory: Cinnamon tea helps boost the immune system, enhancing the body's ability to fight off infections causing coughs, due to the presence of bioactive compounds like polyphenols and flavonoids.
remedy_parts: Bark: The bark of the cinnamon tea plant contains high levels of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, making it effective in soothing coughs and reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Leaves: The leaves of the cinnamon tea plant are rich in essential oils and have expectorant properties, helping to loosen phlegm and relieve congestion in the chest.

Seeds: The seeds of the cinnamon tea plant contain compounds that have been shown to have antitussive and expectorant effects, making them useful in treating coughs and clearing mucus from the respiratory tract.

Roots: The roots of the cinnamon tea plant contain a range of beneficial compounds, including coumarins and tannins, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, making them useful in treating coughs caused by infections.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 cinnamon stick and 1 tablespoon of dried herbal cough-relieving mixture (such as thyme, licorice root, and cherry bark) to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and drink while warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Siberian ginseng tea helps with cough because it contains active compounds called eleutherosides, which have been shown to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation. This herbal tea has expectorant properties that help to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, providing relief from a persistent cough. Additionally, Siberian ginseng tea is known to soothe throat irritation, thereby alleviating the discomfort associated with coughing. Its antiviral and antibacterial properties also help to fight off infections that may cause cough, making it an effective natural remedy for cough relief.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Siberian ginseng tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up phlegm. This is due to the presence of saponins, which have expectorant properties.
Antitussive: The tea can help alleviate coughing by suppressing the cough reflex, thanks to the presence of eleutherosides, which have calming effects on the nervous system.

Anti-inflammatory: Siberian ginseng tea can reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can help to alleviate coughing. This is due to the presence of phenolic compounds, which have anti-inflammatory properties.

Immune Boosting: The tea can help to strengthen the immune system, making the body more resistant to infections that can cause coughing. This is due to the presence of eleutherosides, which have immune-boosting properties.

Adaptogenic: Siberian ginseng tea can help the body to adapt to stress and fatigue, which can often exacerbate a cough. This is due to the presence of eleutherosides, which have adaptogenic properties.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Siberian ginseng tea plant are commonly used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of Siberian ginseng tea plant are used in making medicinal tea for cough as they help boost the immune system and alleviate respiratory symptoms.
Leaves: The leaves of Siberian ginseng tea plant are often used in making medicinal tea for cough as they contain antioxidants that help soothe throat irritation and reduce coughing.
Flowers: The flowers of Siberian ginseng tea plant are used in making medicinal tea for cough as they have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that help relieve respiratory symptoms.
Seeds: The seeds of Siberian ginseng tea plant are used in making medicinal tea for cough as they contain antitussive and expectorant properties that help alleviate cough and expel phlegm.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried Siberian ginseng root.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Remove from heat and add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves and 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root.
Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain and discard the solids.
Sweeten the tea with honey to taste, if desired, and drink it while warm to help soothe a cough.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tasmanian blue gum tea helps with cough because it contains anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The tea is made from the leaves of the Tasmanian blue gum tree, which is native to Australia. The leaves contain compounds called eucalyptus oil and alpha-pinene, which help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and thin mucus, making it easier to expel. Additionally, the tea's soothing warmth can help to alleviate irritation in the throat, providing relief from persistent coughing. Drinking Tasmanian blue gum tea can help to reduce the severity and duration of coughs, making it a natural and effective remedy for respiratory ailments.
remedy_properties: Expectorant property: Tasmanian blue gum tea helps to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel phlegm. This is due to the tea's high content of eucalyptus oil, which has been shown to have expectorant properties.

Antitussive property: The tea can help alleviate coughing by suppressing the cough reflex. This is attributed to the presence of cineole, a compound found in eucalyptus oil, which has been found to have antitussive effects.

Antimicrobial property: Tasmanian blue gum tea can help fight off respiratory infections that may cause coughing. This is due to the tea's antimicrobial properties, which are attributed to the presence of eucalyptus oil and its components, such as 1,8-cineole.

Anti-inflammatory property: The tea can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate coughing. This is due to the anti-inflammatory properties of eucalyptus oil, which has been shown to reduce inflammation in the airways.

Analgesic property: Tasmanian blue gum tea can help relieve the pain and discomfort associated with coughing. This is attributed to the tea's analgesic properties, which are due to the presence of eucalyptus oil and its components, such as 1,8-cineole.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the Tasmanian blue gum tea plant have been traditionally used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-tussive and expectorant properties.
Bark: The bark of the plant is rich in tannins and has been used to soothe irritated throats and reduce coughing.
Flowers: The flowers of the Tasmanian blue gum tea plant contain flavonoids and essential oils, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help alleviate coughing.
Seeds: The seeds of the plant contain essential fatty acids and have been used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing effects on the respiratory tract.
remedy_recipe: In a stove-top kettle, boil 4 cups of water.
Add 2 tablespoons of dried Tasmanian blue gum leaves to a teapot.
Once boiling, pour the water over the leaves and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the leaves.
Drink the tea as needed for cough relief, up to 3 times a day.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eastern purple coneflower tea helps with cough because it contains antitussive and expectorant properties that soothe irritated throats and reduce coughing. This herbal remedy, also known as Echinacea, has been used in traditional medicine to alleviate respiratory symptoms. Its active compounds, such as alkamides and caffeic acid, work together to stimulate the immune system, reduce inflammation, and clear mucus from the airways. As a result, drinking eastern purple coneflower tea can provide relief from both acute and chronic coughs, providing a natural and effective alternative to over-the-counter cough suppressants.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Eastern purple coneflower tea can help alleviate coughing by suppressing the cough reflex, thanks to the presence of alkaloids and flavonoids.
Expectorant: This tea aids in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm and relieve cough, due to its high mucilage content.

Anti-inflammatory: Eastern purple coneflower tea reduces inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can help soothe irritation and ease coughing, thanks to the presence of phenolic acids and flavonoids.
Antimicrobial: The tea can help fight off respiratory infections that may cause coughing, due to its rich content of alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids.

Antioxidant: Eastern purple coneflower tea helps protect the respiratory tract from oxidative damage, which can exacerbate coughing, due to its high concentration of phenolic acids, flavonoids, and other antioxidant compounds.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in antioxidants, the flowers of the eastern purple coneflower are used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Leaves: High in vitamins and minerals, the leaves are used in tea to soothe coughs and alleviate respiratory symptoms, thanks to their natural soothing and relaxing effects.
Stems: Containing bioactive compounds, the stems of the eastern purple coneflower are used in medicinal tea for cough, offering antispasmodic and calming actions that help relieve coughing fits.
Seeds: Packed with essential oils, the seeds are used in tea for their carminative and antitussive properties, helping to expel mucus and reduce coughing.

Roots: Bursting with medicinal compounds, the roots of the eastern purple coneflower are used in tea for cough relief, thanks to their potent expectorant, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory properties.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried eastern purple coneflower herb.
Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 1 minute.
Steep the herb in the hot water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herb pieces.
Drink the tea, up to three times a day, for cough relief.
